overview: Ruby Storm is a fast, mana-dense aggro deck that leverages cheap mana acceleration to cast Seething Song and Desperate Ritual on curve. By flooding the board with low-cost creatures and reducing their costs, it aims to overwhelm opponents before they can stabilize. The inclusion of Manamorphose provides crucial card advantage and mana fixing to keep the aggressive pressure consistent throughout the game.
win_conditions: Overwhelm opponent with multiple cheap creatures on turns two and three
Cast Seething Song early to reduce creature costs and accelerate damage
Use Desperate Ritual to cast multiple spells in a single turn for explosive board presence

keep_criteria: Hands with two or more mana sources and a cheap creature
Hands containing Desperate Ritual or Seething Song with early lands
Hands with Manamorphose and a way to cast it on turn two
Keep 7 with 2-3 lands, 1+ ritual/fixer (Desperate Ritual, Manamorphose), and 1+ cantrip.
Keep 6 with 2 lands, 2+ rituals/fixers, and 1+ cantrip.
mulligan_criteria: Hands with three or more lands and no spells
Hands lacking a turn-one or turn-two play
Hands with only high-cost cards or multiple Manamorphoses
Always mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 0 rituals/fixers, or no wincon/draw engine.

early_game: Prioritize casting Desperate Ritual on turn two to enable a cheap creature and a spell
Look for Seething Song on turn three to flood the board with 1-drops
Play Manamorphose to draw cards and fix mana while maintaining tempo
Turn 1: Cast cantrip or early threat, play land.
Turn 2: Cast ritual or fixer, draw cards, sequence lands for U/R/G access.
Prioritize fixing into Manamorphose/Rituals over untapped lands if possible.
focus on assembling the engine and drawing into the wincon.
mid_game: Maintain board pressure by cycling through cheap threats
Use extra mana to cast multiple creatures or combat tricks in a single turn
Protect key attackers while chipping away at opponent's life total
Treat mana as a resource to be spent, not saved.
Prioritize card advantage over board presence.
The graveyard is a secondary resource for recursion but a liability against hate
manage it by discarding low-value cards or using it to fuel rituals.
Keep hand size low enough to avoid discard but high enough to maintain a wincon.
late_game: If the game stalls, use Manamorphose to find finishers or removal
Leverage mana advantage to outvalue slower decks
Close out games with repeated Seething Song effects or large creature swarms
A high-damage spell (e.g., Grapeshot, Lightning Axe, Tendrils of Agony) cast for zero or negative mana after casting 10+ spells.
Execute by dumping hand into rituals/fixers, then casting the wincon.
Ensure you have enough spells to reach the threshold before committing.
Go all-in when you have a wincon in hand, 1+ mana sources, and a clear path to 10+ spells without the opponent having a guaranteed counter or lethal threat.
If the opponent is at lethal, commit immediately regardless of setup.

key_cards.name: Seething Song
key_cards.notes: Cast on turn three or four to play multiple 1-mana creatures for free, accelerating damage output.

key_cards.name: Desperate Ritual
key_cards.notes: Play on turn two to enable a two-mana creature or spell, setting up explosive turns.

key_cards.name: Manamorphose
key_cards.notes: Use to draw two cards and fix colors, ensuring you can cast Seething Song and Desperate Ritual consistently.

sequencing_tips: Cast Desperate Ritual before playing creatures to maximize mana efficiency
Hold up mana for Seething Song until you have multiple creatures in hand or on board
Play Manamorphose when you need to fix colors or replace a played land
Prioritize casting Seething Song on your opponent's turn if possible to surprise them with a board flood
Common mistakes include overcommitting to a combo turn without checking for counters, miscounting spells, holding too much mana, and ignoring graveyard hate.
Manamorphose fixes into any color but taps for 1 damage
use it wisely to avoid self-damage or missing a critical land drop.
Remember that Storm wins by spell count, so every card drawn matters.
Always count your spells carefully before casting the wincon, and never assume you have enough mana without verifying the exact threshold.

matchups.advice: Play aggressively and commit resources early before they can stabilize
Race them by going wide with cheap creatures rather than trading up
Disrupt their setup with early pressure and prioritize finding interaction

Use cantrips to dig for alternative wincons or disruption. Cast the wincon at end step or during the opponent's turn if possible. Use cheap spells to force counters, then cast the wincon on the following turn. If they have multiple counters, pivot to discard or an uncounterable wincon.

Race them aggressively. Do not let them stabilize. Use discard to hit their mana sources or key threats. If they get ahead, go all-in on turn 3-4 with whatever you have. Prioritize speed over perfect setup.

Use early discard to hit threats or mana. If you have cheap removal or burn, use it to stabilize. If you cannot stabilize, race them with your combo turn. Do not overcommit to board control; Storm wins by going off, not trading.

It is a spell-out war. Prioritize hand disruption (Thoughtseize, Duress) to hit their wincons or rituals. Wincons are often identical, so it comes down to who goes first and who has better disruption. Hold back a ritual if the opponent has a counter, then go off on the following turn.

common_threats: Board wipes that clear your flooded board
Counterspells targeting Seething Song or Desperate Ritual
Life gain effects that slow down your race
Efficient removal that trades favorably with your creatures
Watch for opponents holding mana on their end step or main phase without playing threats—often signals counterspells or discard.
Track graveyard hate, board wipes, and hard counters.
Note when opponents tap out for interaction, as this creates windows to go off safely.
